questions.question: State the Pumping Lemma for regular languages and illustrate its use by proving that L = {a^n b^n | n ≥ 1} is not regular.
keyPoints: Pumping Lemma: If L is regular, ∃ p such that any string w∈L with |w|≥p can be split xyz with |y|>0, |xy|≤p and xy^iz∈L for all i≥0.
Assume L regular, choose w = a^p b^p, apply pumping on y which must lie in a's, derive contradiction.
Show that pumped string cannot maintain equal number of a's and b's, contradicting membership.

questions.question: Explain the Myhill–Nerode theorem and how it can be used to prove that a language is not regular.
keyPoints: Myhill–Nerode: L is regular iff the number of distinct right‑invariant equivalence classes of the relation ~L is finite.
Define equivalence: x ~L y if for all z, xz∈L ⇔ yz∈L.
To show non‑regular, exhibit infinitely many pairwise inequivalent strings (e.g., a^i vs a^j for i≠j for language {a^n b^n}).
Each distinct string yields a different equivalence class, implying L not regular.

questions.question: Prove that the class of regular languages is closed under union, intersection, complement, concatenation, and Kleene star. Provide automaton or expression constructions for each operation.
keyPoints: Union: Construct DFA for L1∪L2 using product construction on DFAs of L1 and L2 (or NFA with ε‑transitions).
Intersection: Same product construction, accepting states are pairs of accepting states of the two DFAs.
Complement: Complement the accept states of a DFA (deterministic and complete) to get ¬L.
Concatenation & Kleene star: Use NFA with ε‑transition from accept states of the first NFA to start of the second for concatenation; add a new start/accept state with loops for star.

questions.question: Using both the Pumping Lemma and the Myhill–Nerode theorem, prove that the language L = {a^n b^n | n ≥ 0} is not regular. Discuss how the Myhill–Nerode equivalence classes demonstrate an infinite number of distinct classes.
keyPoints: Pumping Lemma: Assume regular, pick w = a^p b^p, partition xyz with y in a's, pump to get unequal a and b counts, contradiction.
Myhill–Nerode: For each i≥0 define string a^i; show a^i and a^j (i≠j) are not equivalent because appending b^i yields membership difference.
Thus infinitely many distinct equivalence classes → L not regular.
The proof highlights that any DFA for L would need infinitely many states to distinguish these classes.
